findings.title: Temperature and top_p bounds not enforced in handleSave
findings.description: The HTML number inputs for temperature (min=0, max=2) and top_p (min=0, max=1) only provide browser-level validation. The handleSave function in dialog-edit-agent.tsx calls Number.parseFloat without clamping. A user can type '999' for temperature or '-5' for top_p, and these values will be saved as valid AgentDef values. This could cause unexpected LLM behavior when these extreme parameters are passed to providers.
findings.code_snippet:
const temp = temperature() ? Number.parseFloat(temperature()) : undefined
const tp = topP() ? Number.parseFloat(topP()) : undefined
reproduction_steps: Open dialog-edit-agent for create/edit
Set temperature to '999' by typing in the number field
Click Save
Observe: agent is saved with temperature=999, no error shown
findings.repair_instruction:
Add clamping in handleSave:
- temperature: Math.min(2, Math.max(0, Number.parseFloat(temperature())))
- top_p: Math.min(1, Math.max(0, Number.parseFloat(topP())))
Alternatively, add onInput validation to clamp at the signal level.

findings.title: IPC write-after-write race corrupts electron-store on rapid CRUD
findings.description: saveCustomAgents calls api()?.setCustomAgents?.(agents) which returns a Promise that is NOT awaited. Under rapid operations (e.g., delete agent A then delete agent B), both reads of customAgents() are correct locally (Solid.js batches synchronously), but the IPC writes fire asynchronously. The second IPC write may complete before the first, causing the store to contain stale data. Even if in order, the electron-store.set may not be atomic under concurrent writes — the store state diverges from local state.
reproduction_steps: Create 2 custom agents (A and B)
Rapidly click delete on agent A, then agent B
Each delete calls saveCustomAgents which fires IPC(async)
Close and reopen the app
Observe: either agent may reappear (store has stale write)
findings.repair_instruction: Either: (1) Make saveCustomAgents async and await the IPC, adding a debounce mechanism, or (2) Use a single sequential queue for IPC writes to guarantee ordering. Add a loading state to prevent interactions during store sync.

findings.title: No delete confirmation for custom agents — instant irreversible deletion
findings.description: handleDeleteAgent immediately removes the agent from local state and store with no confirmation dialog. A single accidental click on the trash icon permanently deletes the custom agent definition (including prompt, model config, and all settings) with no undo. This is especially dangerous because the toast notification only shows the deleted name but does not provide an undo action.
reproduction_steps: Open agent management dialog with custom agents
Click the trash icon on any custom agent
Observe: agent is immediately deleted with no confirmation prompt
findings.repair_instruction: Add a confirmation step before deletion. Options: (1) Use a ConfirmationDialog or confirm() prompt, (2) Add toast with undo action button that can restore within a timeout window, (3) Soft-delete with undo period.

findings.title: IPC handlers store.set with no schema validation — type cast bypass corrupts persistence
findings.description: set-desktop-custom-agents IPC handler calls store.set('agents', agents) where agents is typed as unknown[]. No validation checks if the input is a valid array of AgentDef objects. If the renderer accidentally sends null, undefined, or a malformed object (e.g., from a corrupted local state), it's persisted directly. On subsequent reads, get-desktop-custom-agents casts to unknown[] with no runtime check — a non-array value that was persisted will propagate as a type error when iterated.
reproduction_steps: Send IPC with null instead of array: set-desktop-custom-agents null
Observe: store.set('agents', null) writes null to electron-store
Call get-desktop-custom-agents
Observe: (null as unknown[]) passes through, ?? [] returns [] — but if type is object instead of null, the store returns the malformed data
findings.repair_instruction: Add runtime validation in both IPC handlers. In set: validate that input is an array and each element has required AgentDef fields (id, name, prompt). In get: validate the stored value is an array before returning; return [] if not. Consider using a JSON schema validator (like zod) at the IPC boundary.

findings.title: Whitespace-only agent name bypasses empty-name guard
findings.description: Save button is disabled when !name(), but name() returns a truthy value for whitespace-only strings like '   '. User can create/edit agents with invisible names. While the button shows as enabled, this produces an agent with no visible identifier in the list, which may cause confusion and is inaccessible.
reproduction_steps: Open agent create dialog
Type three spaces in the name field
Observe: Save button is enabled (truthy check)
Click Save — agent is created with name='   '
findings.repair_instruction: Change disabled guard to: disabled={!name().trim()}. Also consider trimming the name in handleSave before saving.

findings.title: Side effect in render body — API call on every mount without cleanup
findings.description: The getCustomAgents() IPC call runs in the component function body guarded by `if (!loaded())`. Since `loaded` is a component-local signal, every time the dialog is opened (mounted), loaded() is false, so the API call fires again. For dialog-manage-agents which opens repeatedly, this means repeated IPC calls on every open. Additionally, the .then() callback captures the signal setter in a closure with no unmount guard — if the dialog is closed before the promise resolves, the callback sets state on an unmounted component.
reproduction_steps: Open the Manage Agents dialog
Close it immediately before getCustomAgents resolves
Re-open the dialog
Observe: new API call fires (loaded() is false on new mount)
findings.repair_instruction: Use createEffect + createResource for the API call instead of inline effect. createResource handles cancellation, retry, and cleanup. Alternatively, move the loaded signal to a module-level or context-level store that persists across mounts.

findings.title: Agent list key collision with duplicate names across sources
findings.description: The List component uses key={`${x?.source}:${x?.name}`}. Two custom agents with the same name or a built-in and custom agent with the same name will share the same key. Solid.js and the List component use this as a reconciliation key, causing incorrect DOM reuse (e.g., wrong edit/delete targets, incorrect highlight state).
reproduction_steps: Create a custom agent named 'Build' (same as built-in build agent)
Observe: list key is 'custom:Build' vs 'built-in:Build' — these are unique
Actually: create 2 custom agents with name 'MyAgent'
Observe duplicate key collision
findings.repair_instruction: Use a unique identifier in the key. For built-in agents, use agent ID from SDK. For custom agents, use agent.id (UUID). Change key to `${x?.source}:${x?.agentDef?.id ?? x?.name}`.

findings.title: Null usage (unknown model limit) silently displayed as 0%
findings.description: When no matching provider model is found (limit is undefined), getSessionContextMetrics returns usage: null. In session-context-usage.tsx, `context()?.usage ?? 0` converts null to 0. This displays 0% usage with green color, implying 'no context used' when the truth is 'we don't know the limit'. This is misleading — the user may think they're nowhere near the limit when in fact they could be at 200%.
reproduction_steps: Use a model not registered in providers (custom model, local model without metadata)
Observe: ProgressCircle shows 0% in green
Expected: show '-' or 'N/A' for unknown usage
findings.repair_instruction: Use a sentinel value (null or -1) for unknown usage. In the display, show a dash or 'N/A' instead of 0%. The color should be the default (not green) when usage is unknown. Add a tooltip note: 'Model context limit unknown'.

findings.title: Unbounded message iteration in hasCompaction — O(n) per reactivity tick
findings.description: hasCompaction iterates ALL messages in the session, and for each message looks up sync.data.part[msg.id] and calls .some(). For sessions with thousands of messages, this is O(n * m) where m is the average part count per message. While wrapped in createMemo, ANY change to messages() or sync.data.part triggers a full re-iteration. A compaction event that adds a part to an existing message would trigger re-evaluation of all messages.
reproduction_steps: Open a session with 5000+ messages and frequent compaction events
Observe: every compaction update recalculates hasCompaction over all 5000 messages
Expected: early exit on first compaction match (already done with return true), but still O(n) worst case
findings.repair_instruction: Consider maintaining a compaction set that tracks which message IDs have compaction parts. Update it incrementally when sync.data.part changes instead of scanning all messages. Or add an index on parts by type in the sync data structure.

findings.title: Dead import: createSignal imported but never used
findings.description: session-context-usage.tsx imports createSignal from 'solid-js' but the component never uses it. This wastes module memory, clutters the import graph, and may trigger lint warnings in strict configurations.
findings.code_snippet: import { Match, Show, Switch, createMemo, createSignal } from 'solid-js'
reproduction_steps: Review imports in session-context-usage.tsx
Observe: createSignal is imported and unused
findings.repair_instruction: Remove createSignal from the import statement. It was likely left over from an earlier iteration that used local state but was refactored to use createMemo.

findings.title: No prompt length limit — 128px textarea with 10K chars is unusable
findings.description: The prompt field is a multiline TextField with max-h-32 (128px) and overflow-y-auto. A prompt of 10,000 characters produces a tiny scrollable window. There is no character count, no expand mechanism, and no monospace-only rendering for readability (the font-mono class helps but the container is tiny).
findings.code_snippet:
<TextField
  multiline
  ...
  class='max-h-32 w-full overflow-y-auto font-mono text-xs'
/>
reproduction_steps: Open agent create dialog
Paste a 10,000 character prompt into the prompt field
Observe: scrollable 128px window, no indication of length
findings.repair_instruction: Either: (1) Increase max height or remove max-height constraint for the prompt field, (2) Add an expand/collapse button for the textarea, (3) Add character count indicator, (4) Add validation for reasonable prompt length with a warning message.

findings.title: 100 agents with non-virtualized List causes DOM bloat
findings.description: The List component from @opencode-ai/ui/src/components/list.tsx uses <For> (Solid.js reactive map), not virtualized rendering. With 100 custom agents plus up to 10 built-in agents, the DOM contains 110+ list items with color dots, descriptions, edit/delete buttons, and model labels. This adds ~3000+ DOM nodes for a dialog. While Solid.js handles this efficiently, the rendering time for initial mount grows linearly.
reproduction_steps: Create 100 custom agents via API script
Open agent management dialog
Observe: DOM has 100+ items rendered (no virtualization)
findings.repair_instruction: If 100+ agents is a realistic scenario, consider virtualizing the list or adding pagination. For now, consider this a future concern given the typical number of custom agents.

findings.title: Color set has no default selection and no 'custom color' option
findings.description: The AGENT_COLORS array provides 14 preset colors. There is no default color selected when creating an agent (color starts as ''). The color picker only allows these 14 choices with no custom color input. When no color is selected, the agent list shows a default 'brain' icon instead of a color dot — which is fine functionally but may be unexpected.
reproduction_steps: Create a new agent
Observe: no color pre-selected
Observe: no custom hex input available
findings.repair_instruction: Add a default color for new agents (first in list or a neutral default). Consider adding a color picker or custom hex input for accessibility.

findings.title: Usage percentage can exceed 100% when token total exceeds model limit
findings.description: Math.round((total / limit) * 100) can produce values > 100 when total > limit. For example, 1500 tokens with 1000 limit → 150%. The color thresholds only handle >= 95 (red) but do not differentiate between 'near limit' red and 'over limit' red. A user at 200% usage sees the same red indicator as someone at 96%.
reproduction_steps: Use a model with 1000 context limit
Send messages using 3000 tokens total (exceeding limit)
Observe: usage shows 300%, still red
findings.repair_instruction: Consider capping usage display at 100% with an additional visual indicator (e.g., pulsing icon, exclamation mark) when usage exceeds 100%. The color mapping should distinguish between 'approaching limit' and 'over limit' states.

findings.title: Settings dialog renders 5 tabs — no tab overflow handling
findings.description: The Settings dialog has 5 tabs (General, Shortcuts, Agents, Providers, Models) in two sections. If more tabs were added in the future, there is no overflow or scrolling behavior for the tab list. The tab list is a flex column with fixed layout — tab overflow would push tabs outside the visible area.
reproduction_steps: Observe: 5 tabs fit comfortably in current layout
If 8+ tabs: No overflow mechanism exists
findings.repair_instruction: Consider adding overflow-y-auto to the Tabs.List container for future-proofing. Not actionable now but worth noting for maintenance.

findings.title: IPC getCustomAgents promise rejection silently swallowed
findings.description: Since getCustomAgents() is called without .catch(), a promise rejection (electron-store failure, serialization error) results in an unhandled promise rejection. The .then() callback never fires, so customAgents signal stays as the initial empty array. The user sees an empty custom agents list with no indication that loading failed.
reproduction_steps: Corrupt the electron-store for desktop-custom-agents
Open Manage Agents dialog
Observe: custom agents list is empty with no error indicator
findings.repair_instruction: Add a .catch() handler that either: (1) sets an error signal and shows an error toast, (2) sets customAgents to the empty array gracefully but logs the error. Also consider using createResource which handles errors natively.

findings.title: Agent name in toast description uses unsanitized interpolation
findings.description: The toast description uses `"${agent.name}" created` template literal. If an agent name contains HTML or script content, Solid.js renders it as text (auto-escaped). However, Kobalte's toast component renders through the framework which auto-escapes, so XSS is unlikely. This is informational — no actual vulnerability in Solid.js context.
reproduction_steps: Create agent with name:
Observe: name rendered as text, not HTML (Solid.js auto-escapes)
findings.repair_instruction: No action needed — Solid.js auto-escapes text content. This is informational only to document that potential XSS vectors are blocked by the framework.

findings.title: No session selected (params.id is undefined) — entire component returns nothing
findings.description: SessionContextUsage wraps its entire output in <Show when={params.id}>. When no session is selected, nothing renders — no indicator, no button, no fallback UI. This is intentional behavior (no active session should show no context), but there's no graceful degradation showing 'No session selected' or grayed-out indicator.
reproduction_steps: Open app with no session selected
Observe: no context indicator anywhere
findings.repair_instruction: If the context indicator should always be visible, add a disabled-state fallback inside the Show component. Current behavior may be intentional.

critical_immediate_repairs: STRESS-001: Clamp temperature and top_p in handleSave
STRESS-002: Await IPC writes or use sequential queue for store sync
STRESS-004: Add schema validation in IPC handlers
summary.attack_surface_summary: 3 features attacked across 8 files. 18 findings identified: 4 blockers (all in agent CRUD data integrity path), 6 major (input validation gaps, performance concerns, code quality), 3 minor (UX polish), 5 informational (edge cases with existing guardrails). The Agent Configuration UI has the most severe vulnerabilities — temperature/top_p bounds bypass, IPC race conditions, no delete safety, and unvalidated store writes constitute a data corruption risk.
